steps.title: Identify the logical chain
steps.content:
The passage establishes: (1) medicinal plant knowledge is encoded in Indigenous language vocabulary; (2) as Indigenous languages fade, this knowledge is at risk; (3) tribal nations are preserving their languages. The blank must complete what that preservation also accomplishes — it must follow logically from the language-knowledge link.
  • Choice A — 'increasing the number of medicinal plants represented in the vocabularies of Indigenous languages.': The passage says knowledge is reflected in existing vocabulary — language preservation maintains that knowledge, it doesn't add new plants to the vocabulary. ✗
  • Choice B — 'transmitting terms for medicinal plants from Indigenous languages to globally dominant languages.': The passage focuses on preserving Indigenous languages themselves, not on transferring their vocabulary into English or other dominant languages. ✗
  • Choice C — 'preserving knowledge about the medicinal value of plants native to the tribal nations' lands.': Since medicinal plant knowledge lives in Indigenous language vocabulary, keeping those languages in daily use preserves that knowledge. This follows directly from the passage's logic. ✓
  • Choice D — 'ensuring that citizens of tribal nations have physical access to medicinal plants.': The passage is about linguistic and knowledge preservation, not physical access to plants. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: Language preservation maintains the vocabulary that encodes medicinal plant knowledge, so tribal nations preserving their languages are simultaneously preserving that knowledge. That matches choice C — C is correct.
